title: How to handle the discomfort of being genuinely seen by another person
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
It hits you in the chest, doesn't it? 

 That moment when someone looks at you and actually  sees  you. Not the performance you've been putting on since c...
htmlContent:
It hits you in the chest, doesn't it?

That moment when someone looks at you and actually sees you. Not the performance you've been putting on since childhood. Not the carefully version that keeps you safe. The real you. The one with the cracks and the scars and the parts you've been hiding since before you can remember.

Your throat tightens. Your stomach drops. Every cell in your body screams run.

I know that feeling. I've been there so many times I've lost count. And here's the thing: that discomfort isn't a sign that something's wrong. It's a sign that something's working.

When you've spent years, maybe decades, building armor around your softest parts, being seen feels like an invasion. It feels dangerous. Because for a long time, it was.

Your nervous system learned, probably before you had words for it, that being truly seen meant being vulnerable to harm. Maybe someone used what they saw against you. Maybe your pain was ignored or mocked. Maybe the people who were supposed to protect you couldn't handle what they saw, so you learned to hide.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would call this a neural survival response. Your nervous system scans for safety and danger constantly, below your conscious awareness. And when someone's gaze lands on you with genuine presence, your system might interpret that as a threat. It's not logical. It's biological.

So what do you do when you want connection but your body says danger?

Start by naming what's happening in your body

Before you can do anything about the discomfort, you have to actually feel it. Not think about it. Not analyze it. Not tell yourself a story about why you shouldn't feel this way.

Feel it.

Where is it in your body? Is it a tightness in your chest? A knot in your stomach? A buzzing in your arms and legs that makes you want to bolt? A numbness that feels like you've left your own body?

Stay with me here. I know this part is hard. Your mind will try to pull you into stories, explanations, judgments. I shouldn't feel this way. What's wrong with me? This is ridiculous. Those thoughts are distractions. Come back to the body.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades showing that trauma lives in the body, not in the story. The discomfort of being seen isn't a thought problem. It's a body problem. And body problems require body solutions.

So try this: put your hand on the part of your body that feels uncomfortable. Just rest it there. No agenda. No trying to make it go away. Just presence. You might notice the sensation shift slightly. That's okay. That's good, actually.

If you're someone who struggles with boundaries around being seen, a practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Nedra Glover Tawwab writes about how boundaries aren't walls, they're doors you get to control. And when you know you can close the door, it's easier to let someone peek inside.

You don't have to stay in the discomfort forever. You just have to stay long enough to learn that you survive it. That's the whole game. Bit by bit. Moment by moment.

Let yourself be seen in small doses

You wouldn't run a marathon without training first. Right? So why would you expect yourself to handle hours of deep emotional visibility when your nervous system is still learning that it's safe?

Think about it. You can practice being seen in tiny, manageable increments. A few seconds of eye contact with someone you trust. Sharing one sentence about how you're actually feeling, not the sanitized version. Letting someone notice that you're tired without rushing to explain why.

These small moments matter. They send signals to your nervous system: I'm still safe. Nothing bad happened. I can try again.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. He writes extensively about the fear of being seen, about how survivors often oscillate between desperate longing for connection and terrified retreat from it. Knowing you're not alone in this dance helps. It really does.

Here's what I've learned the hard way: you don't have to be ready. You just have to be willing to try. And if you try and it's too much, you get to stop. That's not failure. That's information.

Your nervous system is telling you its current window of tolerance. Respect that window. Push it gently, but don't smash through it. The goal isn't to force yourself to be comfortable with being seen. The goal is to expand your capacity slowly, with kindness, over time.

The other person's gaze is not a weapon

This is the part that still trips me up sometimes. When someone looks at me with warmth, with genuine interest, with no agenda other than connection, a part of me still braces for impact. Like their eyes are about to turn into something sharp.

But here's what I'm learning: that anticipation of harm is a ghost. It's the echo of old wounds. The person in front of you, if they're safe, is not going to use what they see against you. They're not going to store it up for later. They're not going to mock you or shame you or abandon you because of what they witness.

I know your brain doesn't believe that yet. Mine doesn't always either. That's okay.

Deb Dana, who works extensively with Polyvagal Theory in clinical practice, talks about "glimmers" - those small moments of safety and connection that your nervous system can learn to recognize. A glimmer might be the warmth in someone's voice. The softness in their eyes. The way they don't look away when you show a crack in your armor.

Start collecting glimmers. Notice when being seen doesn't hurt. Notice when it actually feels good, even for a second. Your nervous system needs evidence that safety exists. You can be the one to provide that evidence.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz. It has exercises specifically designed to help you track your nervous system responses and build capacity for connection. Sometimes having a structured path makes the chaos feel more manageable.

You don't have to be completely seen all at once

Let me say this clearly: you are allowed to have parts of yourself that stay private. You are allowed to share only what feels safe to share. Being seen doesn't mean being transparent. It doesn't mean handing someone the keys to every locked room inside you.

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, would say that the parts of you that want to hide are not the enemy. They're protectors. They kept you alive when being seen was dangerous. They deserve gratitude, not shame.

So when the discomfort rises, you can thank that protective part. I see you. I know you're trying to keep me safe. We're okay right now. We can handle this. That internal acknowledgment can shift something. It can turn down the volume on the alarm.

You also get to communicate your needs out loud. I'm feeling a little overwhelmed by this conversation. Can we slow down? Can we take a break? Can you just sit with me without expecting anything? Safe people will respect those requests. Their willingness to honor your pace is part of what makes them safe.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She offers concrete practices for regulating your nervous system so you can stay present even when the discomfort rises. Because the goal isn't to never feel uncomfortable. The goal is to be able to stay in connection when discomfort shows up.

This is a practice, not a destination

I wish I could tell you that one day you'll wake up and being seen will feel easy. That the discomfort will vanish. That you'll stride confidently into every pair of warm, loving eyes without a flicker of fear.

That's not how nervous systems work. That's not how trauma works.

But here's what I can tell you: it gets easier. The moments of being seen that feel safe start to accumulate. Your nervous system starts to learn that not every gaze is a threat. You start to trust yourself to handle the discomfort when it comes.

And somewhere along the way, you might notice something unexpected. You might start to crave being seen. Not in a desperate, hungry way. In a quiet, grounded way. Like a plant turning toward light.

That's not weakness. That's healing.

You are not broken for finding this hard. You are not too much. You are not unworthy of connection. You are a person with a history that taught you to hide, and now you're learning a new way. That takes time. That takes patience. That takes a kind of courage that doesn't make a sound.

So keep practicing. Keep letting yourself be seen in small doses. Keep thanking the parts of you that want to run. Keep collecting glimmers. Keep coming back to your body when the discomfort rises.

And when you can't do any of that, just breathe. That counts too.

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - A resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
